records.ety: [F. endurer; pref. en- (L. in) + durer to last. See Dure, v. i., and cf. Indurate.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Endured (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Enduring (?).]

defs: To continue in the same state without perishing; to last; to remain.
quotes: He shall hold it [his house] fast, but it shall not endure.

defs: To remain firm, as under trial or suffering; to suffer patiently or without yielding; to bear up under adversity; to hold out.
quotes: Can thine heart endure, or can thine hands be strong in the days that I shall deal with thee?

defs: To remain firm under; to sustain; to undergo; to support without breaking or yielding; as, metals endure a certain degree of heat without melting; to endure wind and weather.
quotes: Both were of shining steel, and wrought so pure, As might the strokes of two such arms endure.

defs: To bear with patience; to suffer without opposition or without sinking under the pressure or affliction; to bear up under; to put up with; to tolerate.
quotes: I will no longer endure it.
Therefore I endure all things for the elect's sake.
How can I endure to see the evil that shall come unto my people?

defs: To harden; to toughen; to make hardy.
quotes: Manly limbs endured with little ease.
